symbols: gridiron or iron rack — the instrument of Lawrence’s martyrdom by burning
angel descending with palm branch — the heavenly reward and martyr’s crown
Roman soldiers restraining the saint — worldly power persecuting the faithful
enthroned ruler watching — imperial authority condemning the innocent
Lawrence’s upward gaze — trust in God amid extreme suffering
description: A large dramatic composition showing a young man with a bare torso being forcibly held down on a metal grate or rack by armored Roman soldiers. He gazes upward with an expression of endurance. Above, an angel descends from the clouds carrying a palm branch. To the upper right, a bearded man in a red cloak sits on an elevated platform, observing the scene. A crowd of onlookers fills the background. A tall column dominates the center. The scene is filled with dynamic movement and dramatic chiaroscuro.
theological_description: The Martyrdom of Saint Lawrence, a deacon of the early Church in Rome, occurred in 258 AD under Emperor Valerian. According to tradition, when ordered to hand over the Church’s treasures, Lawrence presented the poor, the crippled, and the suffering, declaring ‘These are the treasures of the Church.’ He was then condemned to death by being roasted on a gridiron. Lawrence’s courage exemplifies the theology of martyrdom rooted in Christ’s words: ‘Whoever loses his life for my sake will find it’ (Matthew 10:39). His defiant identification of the poor as the Church’s true treasure echoes James 2:5: ‘Has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom?’ The angel with the palm branch confirms the promise of Revelation 2:10: ‘Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life.’ Lawrence’s legendary quip on the gridiron—‘Turn me over, I am done on this side’—became a symbol of Christian joy in suffering (Romans 5:3-4).

scripture_refs.reason: Whoever loses his life for Christ’s sake will find it, embodied in Lawrence’s willing martyrdom
Be faithful unto death and receive the crown of life, symbolized by the angel’s palm
God choosing the poor to be rich in faith, echoed in Lawrence’s declaration that the poor are the Church’s treasure
Rejoicing in suffering because suffering produces endurance, character, and hope
